_meta.authoringNotes: WHO visceral leishmaniasis elimination program data. Boelaert M et al. Am J Trop Med Hyg 2019 on amphotericin B efficacy. Sundar S et al. single-dose liposomal amphotericin B (AmBisome) Bihar trials.LEA 2010 diagnostic criteria. Bern C et al. BMJ 2007 systematic review.

h1Title: Visceral Leishmaniasis (Kala-Azar): Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis and Treatment
heroOverview: Visceral leishmaniasis (VL), commonly known as kala-azar (black fever in Hindi), is a vector-borne parasitic disease caused by Leishmania donovani in the Indian subcontinent and East Africa, and by L. infantum (L. chagasi) in the Mediterranean, Middle East, and Latin America. The disease is transmitted by the bite of infected female phlebotomine sandflies and invades the reticuloendothelial system — spleen, liver, bone marrow, and lymph nodes — producing the cardinal clinical features of fever, hepatosplenomegaly, pancytopenia, and weight loss. Without treatment, VL is almost universally fatal, with case fatality rates of 95-100%. Global annual incidence has declined dramatically due to elimination efforts in the Indian subcontinent (from ~200,000 cases/year in the early 2000s to <10,000 in 2023), and East Africa maintains 20,000-30,000 cases annually. HIV-VL co-infection (prevalent in Ethiopia, Sudan, and Brazil) dramatically worsens prognosis, with relapse rates of 50-70% without antiretroviral therapy. Treatment has been transformed by single-dose liposomal amphotericin B (AmBisome 10 mg/kg IV), which achieves >95% cure rates with a single infusion, replacing older toxic regimens (antimonials, amphotericin B deoxycholate). Post-kala-azar dermal leishmaniasis (PKDL) — a cutaneous manifestation developing 1-3 years after VL cure — is the main reservoir in India, complicating elimination efforts.

keyStats.prevalence: Global incidence ~50,000-90,000 per year (WHO 2023); Bihar India accounts for >70% of Indian cases; East Africa ~30,000/year
keyStats.demographics: Predominantly rural poor; children and young adults most commonly affected in East Africa; older males in the Indian subcontinent (often occupationally exposed)
keyStats.avgAge: Median age 15-25 years in East Africa; 30-40 years in Indian subcontinent
keyStats.globalCases: ~50,000-90,000 annual cases globally; eliminated as a public health problem in India, Nepal, Bangladesh since 2023 declaration; residual transmission continues in Bihar and East Africa

definition: Visceral leishmaniasis (ICD-10: B55.0) is a systemic protozoan infection caused by Leishmania donovani complex (L. donovani in Asia and East Africa; L. infantum/chagasi in the Mediterranean and Americas). The parasite exists as an extracellular promastigote in the sandfly vector and as an intracellular amastigote within macrophages of the human host. After inoculation by the sandfly bite, promastigotes are phagocytosed by macrophages and transform into amastigotes, which replicate within the macrophage by binary fission, ultimately rupturing the cell and infecting adjacent macrophages — spreading throughout the reticuloendothelial system (spleen, liver, bone marrow, lymph nodes, and intestinal mucosa). The incubation period ranges from 2 weeks to 18 months (sometimes longer). Clinical VL manifests when the parasite burden overwhelms cell-mediated immunity — requiring a depressed Th1 response (low IFN-γ, IL-2) with elevated IL-4 and IL-10 for disease to progress. HIV co-infection causes rapid progression and treatment failure by further impairing the Th1 immune response. PKDL represents a dermal immune response to persistent L. donovani antigens after systemic cure — appearing as hypopigmented macules, papules, or nodules, typically 1-3 years after VL treatment in Indian cases and earlier (weeks to months) in East African cases. VL is managed by infectious disease specialists, often in the context of public health programs with vector control and surveillance components.

typesClassification.description: The full systemic syndrome: prolonged fever (>2 weeks), marked splenomegaly and hepatomegaly, pancytopenia, weight loss, and characteristic laboratory findings (high globulins, low albumin, positive rK39 rapid test). Case fatality >95% without treatment. Most common presentation in endemic areas.

VL in patients with HIV/AIDS presents atypically — fewer classic symptoms, higher parasite loads, involvement of unusual sites (GI, pulmonary), and relapse rates of 50-70% without ART. Requires combined anti-leishmanial and antiretroviral therapy. Common in Ethiopia, Sudan, and Brazil.

A cutaneous manifestation developing after VL cure. In Indian subcontinent: appears 1-3 years after treatment as hypopigmented macules on face and trunk (may self-resolve or require treatment). In East Africa: appears within weeks to months, papular/nodular, requires treatment. PKDL patients are infectious to sandflies — the main barrier to VL elimination in South Asia.

Rare variant where PKDL lesions involve oral, nasal, or pharyngeal mucosa, producing mucosal ulceration and destruction. Requires more aggressive and prolonged treatment. Can be confused with leprosy or fungal infections.

primarySymptoms: Prolonged fever — typically irregular, biphasic, lasting 2 weeks to several months; often with chills and sweats
Splenomegaly — often massive, causing left upper quadrant discomfort and early satiety; the spleen is firm and non-tender
Hepatomegaly — usually less pronounced than splenomegaly; typically mild-to-moderate enlargement
Weight loss and wasting — progressive, sometimes with muscle loss; severe cachexia in advanced disease
Pallor from anemia — fatigue, dyspnea on exertion from pancytopenia
Lymphadenopathy — particularly in East African VL; firm, non-tender nodes
Skin pigmentation changes — darkening of skin on face, hands, and feet giving 'kala-azar' (black fever) its name; hyperpigmentation may persist after cure
Diarrhea and malabsorption — in advanced disease with intestinal mucosal infiltration
Edema — from hypoalbuminemia (nephrotic-range protein loss) and anemia
Amenorrhea and infertility in women from chronic disease
earlyWarningSigns: Prolonged fever (>2 weeks) with splenomegaly in someone from or traveled to an endemic area
Unexplained weight loss and pallor in a rural endemic area resident
Recurrent fevers without diagnosis despite antibiotic courses — classic presentation missed as 'malaria' or 'typhoid'
Rising anti-leishmanial antibody titers in endemic area surveillance — asymptomatic seroconversion may precede clinical VL
HIV-positive patient from endemic area with fever and organomegaly — VL should be high on differential
emergencySigns: Severe hemorrhage from thrombocytopenia — GI bleeding, intracranial hemorrhage in advanced disease
Co-infection with severe malaria or typhoid fever — simultaneous infections dramatically worsen outcomes
Respiratory distress from severe anemia (hemoglobin <5-6 g/dL) requiring transfusion
Acute kidney injury from immune-complex glomerulonephritis in VL-associated nephrotic syndrome
Profound immunosuppression with secondary bacterial sepsis — common terminal event in untreated VL

causes.cause: Leishmania donovani infection via sandfly bite
causes.description: Female Phlebotomus argentipes (India, Bangladesh, Nepal) or P. orientalis/P. martini (East Africa) transmit L. donovani. The sandfly injects promastigotes during blood feeding; these are phagocytosed by dermal macrophages and disseminate hematogenously.

HIV impairs the Th1 immune response required to contain Leishmania. HIV patients have 100-1000× higher VL risk. VL is an AIDS-defining illness in many countries. ART reduces relapse risk but does not prevent VL in advanced immunosuppression.

Protein-energy malnutrition and micronutrient deficiencies (zinc, vitamin A) impair cell-mediated immunity and increase VL susceptibility and severity. Malnourished children in endemic areas have 3-5× higher VL incidence.

causes.cause: Endemic area residence and sandfly exposure
causes.description: Rural endemic areas with inadequate housing (mud walls, thatched roofs), lack of bed nets, and proximity to cattle and animal shelters increase sandfly exposure and VL risk.

riskFactors.factor: Endemic area residence (Bihar, Jharkhand India; Sudan; Ethiopia)
riskFactors.description: Living in or traveling to known VL-endemic districts dramatically increases exposure risk. Indoor sandfly behavior and nighttime biting patterns make sleeping without bed nets particularly dangerous.
HIV co-infection is the single strongest risk factor for progression from asymptomatic infection to clinical VL, for treatment failure, and for relapse. All VL patients should be HIV tested.
In East Africa, VL predominantly affects children aged 5-14 years, with peak incidence at 8-10 years. In the Indian subcontinent, adults 30-50 years are most affected.
Low body mass index, protein-energy malnutrition, and poverty indicators are associated with increased VL incidence and worse treatment outcomes.
Prior VL provides incomplete immunity; relapse can occur, particularly in HIV co-infected patients. Post-treatment surveillance is essential.

affectedDemographics: Rural poor in endemic areas of the Indian subcontinent, East Africa, and Mediterranean basin
Children 5-14 years in East Africa; adults 30-40 years in Indian subcontinent
HIV/AIDS patients in Ethiopia, Sudan, Brazil — have dramatically different epidemiology (younger, more severe, higher relapse)
diagnosisOverview: VL diagnosis combines clinical suspicion (fever + splenomegaly in endemic area), parasitological confirmation, and serological testing. The rK39 rapid immunochromatographic test (detecting K39 antigen of L. donovani) is the frontline diagnostic in field settings — 97% sensitive and 100% specific in the Indian subcontinent, though slightly less sensitive in East Africa (72-88%) where L. donovani may show antigenic variation. Quantitative direct agglutination test (DAT) is an alternative serological test used in surveillance. Confirmed diagnosis requires parasite demonstration: microscopy of spleen or bone marrow aspirate (spleen aspirate sensitivity 95-99%; bone marrow 60-80%; lymph node aspirate ~60%), or PCR from blood (95% sensitive in blood with specific nested or qPCR protocols). In HIV-VL co-infection, serology can be negative and PCR from tissue is essential. Histopathology of bone marrow shows characteristic Leishmania-Donovan bodies (infected macrophages filled with amastigotes). Liver biopsy shows granulomatous inflammation with parasites. Spleen palpation — measuring the number of centimeters below the costal margin — is a useful clinical marker of disease severity and treatment response.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Point-of-care serological diagnosis of VL in endemic areas. Detects anti-L. donovani IgG antibodies using recombinant K39 antigen.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Finger-prick blood or serum. Results in 15-20 minutes. Sensitivity 97% in Indian subcontinent, 72-88% in East Africa. False positives in other infections (schistosomiasis, leprosy) and healthy individuals in highly endemic areas.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for parasitological confirmation. Direct visualization of Leishmania amastigotes within macrophages.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed by experienced clinician. Classify parasite load on a scale of 1-6+ (WHO). Sensitivity 95-99%. Requires trained staff and carries bleeding risk (thrombocytopenia). Safe when done by experienced hands.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Alternative parasitological confirmation; safer than spleen aspiration for general healthcare workers.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Iliac crest or sternal aspiration. Sensitivity 60-80%. Lower risk of hemorrhage than splenic aspiration. Leishmania-Donovan bodies appear as small ovoid bodies within macrophages.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Highly sensitive and specific molecular diagnosis; essential in HIV co-infection where serology may be negative.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood, bone marrow, or tissue PCR targeting kDNA or small subunit rRNA. Sensitivity 95%+ in peripheral blood. Quantification useful for monitoring treatment response.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Serological test used in surveillance and as alternative to rK39 in East Africa.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Serial serum dilution with Leishmania antigen and visualization of agglutination. Sensitivity 92-94% in East Africa. Requires laboratory setup.

treatmentOverview: Treatment for visceral leishmaniasis is determined by geography, HIV status, and drug resistance patterns. In South Asia, liposomal amphotericin B (AmBisome) at a single dose of 10 mg per kg produces cure rates above 95 percent and has become first-line due to superior efficacy and tolerability over older drug regimens. In East Africa, the combination of sodium stibogluconate plus paromomycin intramuscularly for 17 days remains the first-line regimen in many national programs, though amphotericin B formulations are used for resistance or intolerance. In the Mediterranean basin, where HIV co-infection is common, liposomal amphotericin B is the preferred agent. Miltefosine is an oral drug with reasonable efficacy in South Asia and East Africa, improving treatment accessibility in remote areas, though teratogenicity limits use in women of childbearing age unless contraception is guaranteed. Amphotericin B deoxycholate (0.75 to 1 mg per kg IV daily for 14 to 20 doses) is an effective but more nephrotoxic alternative. Response to treatment is monitored clinically with fever curve and spleen size measurement, and by parasite clearance on serial samples. Relapse requires re-treatment. HIV-positive patients need secondary prophylaxis to prevent relapse after initial cure. Prevention focuses on insecticide-treated bed nets, indoor residual spraying, and early case detection in endemic areas.

medicalTreatments.name: Liposomal amphotericin B (AmBisome) single dose
medicalTreatments.description: The WHO-recommended first-line treatment for VL in South Asia. A single IV infusion of 10 mg/kg achieves >95% cure rates at 6 months follow-up. Transforms VL from a prolonged inpatient treatment to a same-day outpatient procedure in most cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate 95-98% at 6 months in Indian trials (Sundar S et al. NEJM 2011). Comparable efficacy in East Africa with combination (AmBisome + paromomycin). Adverse events: mild infusion reactions (fever, chills) in ~20%, rare nephrotoxicity.
medicalTreatments.typicalCourse: Single IV infusion of 10 mg/kg over 2-4 hours. Patients should be observed for 2-4 hours post-infusion. No additional anti-leishmanial drugs needed in South Asia.

medicalTreatments.name: Liposomal amphotericin B + Paromomycin combination (East Africa)
medicalTreatments.description: WHO first-line for East African VL: single dose AmBisome 10 mg/kg IV plus 7 days of paromomycin 15 mg/kg IM. More effective than antimonials alone for the L. donovani strains in this region.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate 94-97% at 6 months in East African trials. Superior to sodium stibogluconate monotherapy (77-82% cure rate) with less toxicity.
medicalTreatments.typicalCourse: AmBisome 10 mg/kg IV day 1 + Paromomycin 15 mg/kg IM daily for 7 days. Follow-up at 1, 3, 6 months with clinical examination and rK39 monitoring.

medicalTreatments.name: Miltefosine (oral)
medicalTreatments.description: An alkylphosphocholine analog given orally. Effective in Indian VL as monotherapy (28-day course) and in combination with other agents. Not recommended for use in pregnancy (teratogenic). GI side effects common.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate 94-96% as monotherapy in Indian trials (Sundar S et al. Lancet 2004). Lower efficacy in East Africa (~85%). Emerging resistance in some areas.
medicalTreatments.typicalCourse: 2.5 mg/kg/day orally in divided doses for 28 days (adults: 50 mg twice daily; children: weight-based). Monitor renal function and GI tolerance. Teratogenic — strict contraception required during and 5 months after treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Pentavalent antimonials (sodium stibogluconate)
medicalTreatments.description: The traditional VL treatment, now largely replaced in South Asia by AmBisome. Still used in East Africa and some parts of Brazil where AmBisome is not yet fully scaled up.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate 77-85% in East Africa with 30-day course. High failure rates in Bihar (up to 65% failures) due to antimonial resistance. Significant toxicity limits use.
medicalTreatments.typicalCourse: 20 mg Sb/kg/day IV or IM for 20-30 days. Monitor ECG for QT prolongation and cardiac arrhythmias; check amylase/lipase for pancreatitis; liver function tests.

surgicalOptions.name: Splenectomy for refractory VL
surgicalOptions.description: Rarely required in the modern era. Reserved for end-stage VL with massive splenomegaly, severe pancytopenia, and treatment failure. Can be curative alone (parasites primarily in spleen), but requires careful patient selection.
surgicalOptions.whatToExpect: Major abdominal surgery in a malnourished, pancytopenic patient with high surgical risk. Outcomes can be excellent — spleen harbors most parasites. Post-splenectomy prophylaxis with penicillin and vaccination against encapsulated organisms required.

alternativeTreatments.name: Amphotericin B deoxycholate
alternativeTreatments.description: The non-liposomal formulation of amphotericin B. Effective but nephrotoxic; requires 15-20 daily IV infusions.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate 95%+ but requires hospitalization and monitoring. Renal toxicity in 20-30% of patients.
alternativeTreatments.typicalCourse: 0.5-1 mg/kg IV daily for 15-20 days. Requires hospitalization, renal monitoring, pre-hydration.

alternativeTreatments.name: Pentamidine isethionate
alternativeTreatments.description: An alternative for antimonial failures or where AmBisome is unavailable. Used in Brazil and as secondary prophylaxis in HIV-VL.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate 85-90%. Less effective than AmBisome. Hypoglycemia and cardiac toxicity limit use.
alternativeTreatments.typicalCourse: 4 mg/kg IV/IM daily for 7 days. Monitor blood glucose (hypoglycemia) and ECG.

alternativeTreatments.name: Paromomycin monotherapy (IM)
alternativeTreatments.description: An aminoglycoside with anti-leishmanial activity. Used in combination regimens in East Africa. Less effective as monotherapy in South Asia.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate 85-90% as monotherapy in East Africa. Inferior to AmBisome in India.
alternativeTreatments.typicalCourse: 15 mg/kg IM daily for 21 days. Monitor hearing and renal function.

whySeeSpecialist: VL requires an infectious disease specialist for diagnosis confirmation (parasitological diagnosis), treatment selection (which depends on region, resistance patterns, HIV status), and management of complications. VL in HIV patients requires coordination with HIV specialists for ART optimization. PKDL requires dermatology input. Public health notification is required in most endemic countries. Vector control teams should be engaged for environmental management.
doctorSelectionGuide: In endemic areas (Bihar, Jharkhand, West Bengal in India; Sudan, Ethiopia, Kenya in East Africa), government-designated VL treatment centers have staff experienced in VL management. In non-endemic settings, an infectious disease specialist at a referral hospital can manage VL, typically in consultation with CDC or WHO regional leishmaniasis reference centers.
hospitalCriteria: Infectious disease department with VL diagnosis and management experience
Laboratory capable of rK39 rapid test, bone marrow/splenic aspiration microscopy, and Leishmania PCR
IV amphotericin B formulation capability (liposomal or deoxycholate)
Blood bank for transfusion support in severe anemia
Public health notification system for leishmaniasis surveillance
keyFacilities: Microscopy center for tissue aspirate examination (Giemsa-stained slides)
PCR laboratory for molecular diagnosis (reference lab acceptable)
Pharmacy with AmBisome, miltefosine, and antimonial preparations
Radiology for ultrasound (splenic size assessment) and monitoring
Nutrition support service for malnourished VL patients
HIV testing and ART initiation service

costBreakdown.consultation: Infectious disease consultation: ₹300-₹1,500. VL treatment in government facilities in India is often free through the National Vector Borne Disease Control Programme (NVBDCP).
costBreakdown.diagnosticTests: rK39 RDT: free in government facilities; ₹200-₹500 in private. Spleen/bone marrow aspiration: ₹300-₹1,000. PCR: ₹1,500-₹5,000 at reference labs.
costBreakdown.medications: AmBisome single dose (10 mg/kg): approximately ₹5,000-₹15,000 ($60-$180) for average adult; provided free in government programs. Miltefosine 28-day course: ₹3,000-₹8,000 ($35-$95).
costBreakdown.surgery: Splenectomy: ₹20,000-₹60,000 if required (rare in modern era).

insuranceGuide: In India, VL treatment is provided free under the National Vector Borne Disease Control Programme (NVBDCP) at government health facilities. Ayushman Bharat PMJAY covers hospitalization for severe VL in eligible families. International travelers to endemic areas should check travel insurance for tropical disease coverage.
financialAssistance: WHO and DNDi (Drugs for Neglected Diseases initiative) support AmBisome access in endemic countries. NVBDCP India provides free diagnosis and treatment. MSF (Médecins Sans Frontières) runs VL treatment programs in East Africa. Some pharmaceutical patient access programs exist for miltefosine.
preventionStrategies: Indoor residual spraying with long-acting insecticides in endemic districts (NVBDCP in India)
Sleeping under insecticide-treated bed nets (ITNs) — sandflies bite at night
Indoor residual spraying of houses in endemic areas — reduces sandfly density dramatically
Early detection and complete treatment of VL cases and PKDL cases — reduces transmission
Health education in endemic villages about sandfly behavior and prevention
Vector control: improving housing (sealing mud cracks in walls), clearing vegetation near houses, using bed nets
lifestyleModifications: Sleep under insecticide-treated bed nets every night in endemic areas
Use indoor residual insecticide spray on house walls annually
Avoid sleeping on the floor — use raised beds
Keep cattle and animal shelters away from living quarters
Use insect repellent on exposed skin in evening hours (sandflies bite dusk to dawn)
Wear long sleeves and trousers in the evening in endemic areas

recommended: High-protein diet to reverse wasting and support immune function — eggs, fish, chicken, legumes
Iron-rich foods for anemia — red meat, liver, lentils, spinach, dates
Vitamin A and zinc supplementation — both support cell-mediated immunity against Leishmania
Small, frequent meals if early satiety from splenomegaly limits food intake
Oral rehydration with ORS during febrile episodes
avoid: Raw or undercooked meat that could be contaminated — important in immunocompromised patients
Alcohol which worsens liver dysfunction common in VL
High-fat meals immediately before or after miltefosine (worsens GI side effects)

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is not appropriate during active VL with fever, significant splenomegaly, and pancytopenia. Rest is essential during the acute illness. After cure and return of strength (typically 4-8 weeks post-treatment), gradual return to normal activities including light exercise is encouraged. No specific exercises prevent VL — vector avoidance is the only effective prevention.
dailyManagement: Complete the full course of anti-leishmanial medication — incomplete treatment leads to relapse and drug resistance
Sleep under insecticide-treated bed nets year-round in endemic areas
Monthly follow-up for 6 months post-treatment to detect relapse early
HIV testing at VL diagnosis and, if HIV-positive, start ART promptly and continue secondary prophylaxis
Monitor for PKDL development — hypopigmented patches or nodules appearing months after cure; report to health center
Monitor weight and appetite — improvement in these is the best clinical indicator of treatment response
Family members in the same household should be screened (rK39 test) for asymptomatic infection
Report any fever occurring within 6 months of treatment completion — may indicate relapse requiring retreatment
prognosis: Without treatment, visceral leishmaniasis is almost universally fatal within 2 years. With appropriate therapy, cure rates exceed 90% in immunocompetent patients in South Asia and East Africa. Relapse occurs in 5–10% of treated patients, often associated with incomplete initial parasite clearance or underlying immune suppression. HIV co-infected patients experience higher relapse rates of 50–65% without secondary prophylaxis, making maintenance therapy critical. Post-kala-azar dermal leishmaniasis — a cutaneous manifestation appearing in 5–10% of VL cases in East Africa after treatment — requires separate management with amphotericin B or miltefosine. Overall VL mortality has declined with the introduction of liposomal amphotericin B combination therapies in national programs, though access to treatment in remote endemic regions remains limited.
recoveryTimeline: Fever usually resolves within 1-2 weeks of starting effective treatment. Splenomegaly regresses over 4-8 weeks. Hemoglobin recovers over 2-3 months. Weight gain is slow but progressive over 3-6 months. Most patients feel well and return to normal activities within 4-8 weeks. Surveillance for relapse continues for 6 months post-treatment. PKDL may appear 1-3 years after VL cure and requires its own treatment course.
complications: Severe anemia requiring blood transfusion — hemoglobin often drops to 4-6 g/dL in advanced VL
Hemorrhage from thrombocytopenia — GI bleeding or cutaneous bleeding; can be fatal
Secondary bacterial infections (sepsis) — Salmonella, tuberculosis co-infection common in malnourished patients
Co-infection with HIV — dramatically worsens prognosis, increases relapse, and transforms VL into an AIDS-defining condition
Post-kala-azar dermal leishmaniasis (PKDL) — appears months to years after cure; requires separate treatment course; infectious to sandflies

supportResources.name: WHO Leishmaniasis Programme
supportResources.description: Global guidance on VL diagnosis, treatment, surveillance, and elimination programs. Updated treatment guidelines 2022.

supportResources.name: NVBDCP India (National Vector Borne Disease Control Programme)
supportResources.description: India's national VL elimination program providing free diagnosis, treatment, and vector control in endemic states.

supportResources.name: DNDi (Drugs for Neglected Diseases initiative)
supportResources.description: Non-profit drug development organization working on improved VL treatments and access.

confusedWithConditions.name: Malaria with splenomegaly
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause fever and splenomegaly in endemic areas. Malaria shows Plasmodium parasites on blood smear and typically resolves with antimalarials. VL has a negative malaria smear, positive rK39 test, higher globulins, and progressive wasting not seen in malaria.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic myeloid leukemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: CML causes splenomegaly and hepatomegaly, but has characteristic peripheral blood findings (left shift, basophilia, high WBC), positive BCR-ABL PCR, and lacks the fever, weight loss, and positive rK39 of VL.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic malaria with splenomegaly
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hyperreactive malarial splenomegaly (HMS) from chronic malaria can mimic VL — massive splenomegaly, anemia, high IgM. Distinguished by negative rK39, very high IgM, no Leishmania amastigotes on aspirate, and response to antimalarial/immunosuppression.

coOccurringConditions.name: HIV/AIDS
coOccurringConditions.reason: HIV co-infection is the strongest prognostic factor for VL — dramatically increases susceptibility, treatment failure, and relapse. Both infections should be treated simultaneously with ART and anti-leishmanial therapy.

coOccurringConditions.name: Tuberculosis
coOccurringConditions.reason: TB is a common co-infection in malnourished VL patients, particularly in East Africa. TB treatment should be started if present; immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) can occur when ART is started in VL-HIV patients.

coOccurringConditions.name: Schistosomiasis
coOccurringConditions.reason: Co-infection with schistosomiasis in East Africa can cause false-positive rK39 results due to cross-reactive antibodies; tissue biopsy may be needed for definitive diagnosis.

relatedConditions.name: Post-kala-azar dermal leishmaniasis (PKDL)
relatedConditions.relationship: A cutaneous manifestation developing after VL cure; the main transmission reservoir in South Asia elimination program

relatedConditions.name: Cutaneous leishmaniasis
relatedConditions.relationship: Self-limiting skin lesions from L. major/tropica; does not progress to VL in immunocompetent individuals

relatedConditions.name: Mucocutaneous leishmaniasis
relatedConditions.relationship: Destructive mucosal disease from L. braziliensis complex; requires different treatment approach than VL

faqs.question: What is visceral leishmaniasis and how is it different from cutaneous leishmaniasis?
faqs.answer: Visceral leishmaniasis (kala-azar) affects the internal organs — spleen, liver, bone marrow — and is fatal without treatment. It causes fever, massive splenomegaly, weight loss, and anemia. Cutaneous leishmaniasis causes only skin ulcers that usually heal on their own. The two diseases are caused by different Leishmania species and require different treatments. Only VL can spread to internal organs.

faqs.question: How is visceral leishmaniasis transmitted?
faqs.answer: VL is transmitted by the bite of infected female sandflies — tiny insects (2-3 mm) that live in mud walls, thatched roofs, and animal shelters. They bite at night, between dusk and dawn. The sandfly injects Leishmania parasites when it feeds on blood. There is no person-to-person transmission of VL directly — the sandfly is always required.

faqs.question: What are the first symptoms of kala-azar?
faqs.answer: The first symptoms are usually fever (often irregular and lasting weeks), feeling tired and weak, weight loss, and an enlarged spleen (you may feel a lump under your left ribs). As the disease progresses, the skin may darken — 'kala-azar' means 'black fever' in Hindi. Patients look pale (anemic) and very thin. Without treatment, these symptoms worsen until death.

faqs.question: Is there a vaccine against leishmaniasis?
faqs.answer: No licensed vaccine exists for human leishmaniasis. Several vaccine candidates are in development — including a recombinant LEISH-F3+ MPL-SE vaccine in trials in India and Brazil. Prevention currently relies on vector control (bed nets, insecticide spraying) and early case detection and treatment.

faqs.question: How is visceral leishmaniasis treated?
faqs.answer: The best modern treatment is a single injection of liposomal amphotericin B (AmBisome) — a 10 mg/kg IV infusion that cures >95% of patients in one dose. This has replaced the older 30-day injections of antimonial drugs that were very painful, toxic, and required hospitalization. Miltefosine (oral) and combination therapies are also used in different regions.

faqs.question: Is visceral leishmaniasis completely curable?
faqs.answer: Yes, with modern treatment (single-dose AmBisome), >95% of patients are completely cured. However, treatment must be completed fully. Relapse can occur — especially in HIV co-infected patients — and requires retreatment. Some patients develop PKDL (skin lesions) after being cured, which requires separate treatment.

faqs.question: What is PKDL and why does it matter?
faqs.answer: PKDL (post-kala-azar dermal leishmaniasis) is a rash that appears months or years after VL is cured — hypopigmented patches or nodules on the face and body. It doesn't make the patient sick but the skin lesions contain Leishmania parasites that can infect sandflies. In India, PKDL patients are the main transmission reservoir preventing VL elimination. Treating PKDL is therefore a public health priority, not just a medical one.

faqs.question: How can I prevent getting VL if I live in or travel to an endemic area?
faqs.answer: Sleep under insecticide-treated bed nets every night (sandflies bite between dusk and dawn). Use indoor residual insecticide spray on walls of your house. Wear long sleeves and trousers in the evening. Keep animal shelters away from living areas. Improve housing to eliminate sandfly resting sites (cracks in mud walls, thatch). Report any prolonged fever to the nearest health center promptly.

faqs.question: Is VL common in India and where are the hotspots?
faqs.answer: India accounts for the majority of VL cases in South Asia. The state of Bihar (particularly the districts of Vaishali, Muzaffarpur, Samastipur, Begusarai, and Saharsa) is the epicenter — accounting for >70% of Indian cases. Jharkhand and West Bengal are other endemic states. India, Nepal, and Bangladesh have together committed to VL elimination (defined as <1 case per 10,000 population at district level) and have reduced cases by >95% since the early 2000s.

faqs.question: What is the connection between HIV and VL?
faqs.answer: HIV infection dramatically increases the risk of getting VL — people with AIDS are 100-1000 times more likely to develop VL. HIV-VL co-infection is very difficult to treat, with high relapse rates (50-70%) and requires lifelong anti-leishmanial prophylaxis alongside ART. Countries with the highest HIV-VL co-infection rates are Ethiopia, Sudan, and Brazil.

faqs.question: How is VL diagnosed?
faqs.answer: The first test is a finger-prick blood test called rK39 rapid diagnostic test — results in 20 minutes at a health center. If positive, the doctor may take a small sample from your spleen or bone marrow (with a needle) to look for the Leishmania parasite under a microscope — this confirms the diagnosis. PCR testing of blood or tissue is the most sensitive test, especially in HIV co-infection.

faqs.question: Can VL come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes, relapse can occur, particularly in HIV co-infected patients (50-70% relapse rates). Relapse usually occurs within 6 months of treatment and is treated again with AmBisome or combination therapy. After treatment, you should be monitored monthly for 6 months. Report any return of fever or splenomegaly to your health center immediately.

metaDescription: Visceral leishmaniasis (kala-azar): sandfly-borne parasitic disease. Symptoms, amphotericin B treatment, and prognosis reviewed by our medical board.
